content:
# Numbers Chapter 11 — The EMET Bible (Literal)

**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pp. 160–162; Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 120–121 (LXX) | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/NUM_011_page_001.jpg`, `NUM_011_page_002.jpg`, `NUM_011_page_003.jpg`; `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/NUM_011_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `NUM_011_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

> **MANUSCRIPT OBSERVATION — FROM SINAI TO REBELLION: THE FIRST CRISIS OF THE WILDERNESS:** Numbers 11 marks the decisive turn from the ordered departure of chapter 10 to the crisis-narratives that will dominate Numbers 11–25. The chapter contains three interlocking episodes: the fire at Taberah (vv. 1–3), the craving for meat and Moses' burden (vv. 4–30), and the quail and plague (vv. 31–35). Structurally, the chapter moves from the people's complaint to YHWH's anger, to Moses' complaint to YHWH, to YHWH's dual response — distributing the spirit upon seventy elders (vv. 16–17, 24–30) and sending quail followed by plague (vv. 31–34). The Leningrad Codex presents this chapter across pages 160–162, beginning immediately after the inverted-nun passage that concluded chapter 10. The Vaticanus LXX preserves the chapter on pages 120–121 (pa_0120 to pa_0121), following the MT closely with some notable lexical choices. The open parashah marker (פ) appears before v. 4, separating the Taberah incident from the longer quail narrative. A setumah (ס) appears at the end of the chapter before 12:1.

---

## The Fire at Taberah (vv. 1–3)

**1** And-it-was [va-yehi] the-people [ha-am] as-ones-complaining-of-evil [ke-mit'onenim ra] in-the-ears of [be-oznei] YHWH; and-heard [va-yishma] YHWH, and-burned [va-yikhar] his-anger [appo], and-burned [va-tiv'ar] among-them [bam] [the]-fire of [esh] YHWH, and-consumed [va-tokhal] on-the-edge of [bi-qetseh] the-camp [ha-makhaneh].

> **Key terms:** מִתְאֹנְנִים (mit'onenim) = "ones complaining" — a hitpoel participle from אנן, a rare form appearing only here in the Hebrew Bible. The root suggests murmuring, groaning, or lamenting. The word רַע (ra, "evil") modifies the complaint — they were complaining of evil, i.e., complaining about hardship or grievance. The construction כְּמִתְאֹנְנִים (ke-mit'onenim) with the preposition כְּ ("as/like") is notable: the people were "as ones complaining" — perhaps suggesting YHWH perceived their attitude even before it fully manifested. בִּקְצֵה הַמַּחֲנֶה (bi-qetseh ha-makhaneh) = "on the edge of the camp" — the fire consumed the periphery, not the center where the Tabernacle stood. **LXX:** Καὶ ἦν ὁ λαὸς γογγύζων πονηρὰ ἔναντι κυρίου ("and the people were murmuring evil things before [the] Lord") — rendering מִתְאֹנְנִים with γογγύζων (gonguzōn, "murmuring/grumbling"), the same verb used for Israel's wilderness complaints in Exodus 15–17.

**2** And-cried [va-yits'aq] the-people [ha-am] unto [el] Moses [Mosheh]; and-prayed [va-yitpallel] Moses [Mosheh] unto [el] YHWH, and-sank-down [va-tishqa] the-fire [ha-esh].

> **Key terms:** וַיִּצְעַק (va-yits'aq) = "and cried out" — from צעק (tsa'aq, "to cry out/shriek"), expressing desperate appeal. The people turn to Moses as mediator. וַיִּתְפַּלֵּל (va-yitpallel) = "and he prayed" — the hitpael of פלל. Moses' intercession is effective: the fire subsides. וַתִּשְׁקַע (va-tishqa) = "and it sank down/subsided" — from שׁקע (shaqa', "to sink, subside, abate"). The same verb describes waters subsiding (cf. the related noun in Amos 8:8). **LXX:** καὶ ἐκέκραξεν ὁ λαὸς πρὸς Μωυσῆν, καὶ ηὔξατο Μωυσῆς πρὸς κύριον, καὶ ἐκόπασεν τὸ πῦρ ("and the people cried out to Moses, and Moses prayed to [the] Lord, and the fire abated") — using ἐκόπασεν (ekopasen, "it ceased/abated") for וַתִּשְׁקַע.

**3** And-he-called [va-yiqra] the-name of [shem] that [ha-hu] place [ha-maqom] Taberah [Tav'erah], for [ki] burned [ba'arah] among-them [bam] [the]-fire of [esh] YHWH.

> **Key terms:** תַּבְעֵרָה (Tav'erah) = "Burning" — an etiological name derived from בער (ba'ar, "to burn/consume"). The naming formula follows the standard pattern: event → place-name → explanatory כִּי-clause. This site is mentioned again only in Deuteronomy 9:22. **LXX:** καὶ ἐκλήθη τὸ ὄνομα τοῦ τόπου ἐκείνου Ἐμπυρισμός ("and the name of that place was called 'Burning'") — translating the Hebrew name rather than transliterating it, rendering תַּבְעֵרָה as Ἐμπυρισμός (Empurismos, "Conflagration/Burning").

---

## The Craving for Meat: The People's Complaint and Moses' Burden (vv. 4–15)

**4** And-the-mixed-multitude [ve-ha-asafsuf] that [asher] in-their-midst [be-qirbo] craved [hit'avvu] a-craving [ta'avah]; and-returned [va-yashuvu] and-wept [va-yivku] also [gam] the-Sons of [benei] Israel [Yisra'el], and-said [va-yomeru]: "Who [mi] will-feed-us [ya'akhilenu] flesh [basar]?

> **Key terms:** הָאסַפְסֻף (ha-asafsuf) = "the mixed multitude/rabble" — a hapax legomenon, formed by reduplication of אסף (asaf, "to gather/collect"), yielding a pejorative sense: "the gathered-gathering," i.e., the riffraff, the collected mob. These are likely the עֵרֶב רַב (erev rav, "great mixture") of Exodus 12:38 — non-Israelites who joined the exodus. הִתְאַוּוּ תַּאֲוָה (hit'avvu ta'avah) = "craved a craving" — a cognate accusative construction (verb + noun from the same root אוה), intensifying the desire. The craving spreads from the mixed multitude to the Sons of Israel themselves. **LXX:** καὶ ὁ ἐπίμικτος ὁ ἐν αὐτοῖς ἐπεθύμησεν ἐπιθυμίαν ("and the mixed [multitude] among them desired a desire") — ἐπίμικτος (epimiktos, "mixed") translates the Hebrew concept of a foreign element within the camp.

**5** We-remember [zakharnu] [et] the-fish [ha-dagah] that [asher] we-ate [nokhal] in-Egypt [be-Mitsrayim] for-nothing [khinnam]; [et] the-cucumbers [ha-qishshu'im] and-[et] the-melons [ha-avattikkim] and-[et] the-leeks [he-khatsir] and-[et] the-onions [ha-betsalim] and-[et] the-garlic [ha-shumim].

> **Key terms:** חִנָּם (khinnam) = "for nothing/freely/gratis" — this detail reveals the distortion of memory. The fish may have been cheap or readily available along the Nile, but life in Egypt was hardly "free" — they were slaves. The list of five vegetables constitutes the most detailed Egyptian food catalogue in the Torah: (1) קִשֻּׁאִים (qishshu'im) = "cucumbers"; (2) אֲבַטִּחִים (avattikkim) = "melons/watermelons"; (3) חָצִיר (khatsir) = "leeks" (lit. "grass/green herb," here specifically leeks); (4) בְּצָלִים (betsalim) = "onions"; (5) שׁוּמִים (shumim) = "garlic." All five are attested in ancient Egyptian agriculture. **LXX:** ἐμνήσθημεν τοὺς ἰχθύας οὓς ἠσθίομεν ἐν Αἰγύπτῳ δωρεάν, τοὺς σικύους καὶ τοὺς πέπονας καὶ τὰ πράσα καὶ τὰ κρόμμυα καὶ τὰ σκόρδα ("we remembered the fish that we ate in Egypt freely, the cucumbers and the melons and the leeks and the onions and the garlic").

**6** And-now [ve-attah] our-soul [nafshenu] [is] dried-up [yeveishah]; there-is-nothing [ein kol] — except [bilti] unto [el] the-manna [ha-man] our-eyes [einenu] [look]."

> **Key terms:** נַפְשֵׁנוּ יְבֵשָׁה (nafshenu yeveishah) = "our soul [is] dried up" — the נֶפֶשׁ here denotes appetite/vitality. The dryness is a metaphor for the absence of desire-satisfying food. אֵין כֹּל בִּלְתִּי אֶל־הַמָּן עֵינֵינוּ (ein kol bilti el ha-man einenu) = "there is nothing at all — except unto the manna [are] our eyes" — a striking expression of monotonous deprivation: they see nothing but manna wherever they look. **LXX:** νυνὶ δὲ ἡ ψυχὴ ἡμῶν κατάξηρος, οὐδὲν πλὴν εἰς τὸ μαννα οἱ ὀφθαλμοὶ ἡμῶν ("but now our soul [is] utterly dry, [there is] nothing except toward the manna [are] our eyes").

**7** And-the-manna [ve-ha-man] [was] like-the-seed of [ke-zera] coriander [gad], and-its-appearance [ve-eino] like-the-appearance of [ke-ein] bdellium [ha-bedolakh].

> **Key terms:** גַּד (gad) = "coriander" — a small, round, whitish seed (cf. Exod 16:31, where the manna is described as "like coriander seed, white"). בְּדֹלַח (bedolakh) = "bdellium" — a translucent, yellowish-white aromatic resin, or possibly a precious stone (cf. Gen 2:12). The comparison suggests the manna had a pale, lustrous appearance. This parenthetical description (vv. 7–9) interrupts the complaint to remind the reader what the manna actually was. **LXX:** τὸ δὲ μαννα ὡσεὶ σπέρμα κορίου ἐστίν, καὶ τὸ εἶδος αὐτοῦ εἶδος κρυστάλλου ("and the manna was as the seed of coriander, and its appearance [was the] appearance of crystal") — notably rendering בְּדֹלַח as κρύσταλλος (krustallos, "crystal/ice") rather than transliterating.

**8** They-went-about [shatu] the-people [ha-am] and-gathered [ve-laqetu], and-ground [ve-takhanu] on-the-mill [ba-rekhayim] or [o] crushed [dakhu] in-the-mortar [ba-medokhah], and-cooked [u-visshelu] in-the-pot [ba-parur], and-made [ve-asu] [et]-it [oto] [into] cakes [ugot]; and-was [ve-hayah] its-taste [ta'amo] like-the-taste of [ke-ta'am] a-cake of [leshad] oil [ha-shamen].

> **Key terms:** שָׁטוּ (shatu) = "they went about/roamed" — from שׁוט (shut, "to go about, roam"). The manna had to be actively collected. The preparation sequence involves four methods: (1) טָחַן (takhan, "to grind") on a רֵחַיִם (rekhayim, "hand-mill/millstones"); (2) דָּכָה (dakhah, "to crush/pound") in a מְדֹכָה (medokhah, "mortar"); (3) בִּשֵּׁל (bishshel, "to cook/boil") in a פָּרוּר (parur, "pot/cooking vessel"); (4) עָשָׂה (asah, "to make") into עֻגוֹת (ugot, "cakes/round bread"). לְשַׁד הַשָּׁמֶן (leshad ha-shamen) = "a cake of oil" or "the richness of oil" — לְשַׁד is debated; it may mean "moisture/freshness" or a type of pastry made with oil. **LXX:** καὶ διεπορεύετο ὁ λαὸς καὶ συνέλεγον καὶ ἤληθον αὐτὸ ἐν τῷ μύλῳ ... ἐγκρὶς ἐξ ἐλαίου ("and the people went about and collected and ground it in the mill ... a cake of oil").

**9** And-when-descended [u-ve-redeth] the-dew [ha-tal] upon [al] the-camp [ha-makhaneh] at-night [lailah], descended [yered] the-manna [ha-man] upon-it [alav].

> **Key terms:** The manna arrived with the nocturnal dew — a detail consistent with Exodus 16:13–14, where the dew layer covered the ground and the manna lay beneath it. The verb יָרַד (yarad, "to descend/come down") links the manna to heaven; it descends from above, not rising from the earth. **LXX:** καὶ ὅταν κατέβη ἡ δρόσος ἐπὶ τὴν παρεμβολὴν νυκτός, κατέβαινεν τὸ μαννα ἐπ᾽ αὐτῆς ("and when the dew came down upon the camp by night, the manna came down upon it").

**10** And-heard [va-yishma] Moses [Mosheh] [et] the-people [ha-am] weeping [bokheh] by-their-families [le-mishpekhotav], each [ish] [at]-the-entrance of [petakh] his-tent [oholo]; and-burned [va-yikhar] the-anger of [af] YHWH exceedingly [me'od]; and-in-the-eyes of [u-ve-einei] Moses [Mosheh] [it was] evil [ra].

> **Key terms:** בֹּכֶה לְמִשְׁפְּחֹתָיו (bokheh le-mishpekhotav) = "weeping by their families" — the weeping was organized, familial, and public; each household wept at the entrance of its tent. This was not spontaneous grief but a collective demonstration. The phrase אִישׁ לְפֶתַח אָהֳלוֹ (ish le-petakh oholo) = "each [man] at the entrance of his tent" evokes the camp layout where each family had its designated tent-opening facing outward. The double reaction — YHWH's anger burning and Moses seeing [it as] evil — sets up the twin crises that follow: YHWH's anger resolves in quail and plague (vv. 31–34), and Moses' distress resolves in the seventy elders (vv. 16–17, 24–25). **LXX:** καὶ ἤκουσεν Μωυσῆς κλαιόντων αὐτῶν κατὰ δήμους αὐτῶν, ἕκαστον ἐπὶ τῆς θύρας αὐτοῦ ("and Moses heard them weeping by their clans, each at his door").

**11** And-said [va-yomer] Moses [Mosheh] unto [el] YHWH: "Why [lamah] have-you-done-evil [hare'ota] to-your-servant [le-avdekha]? And-why [ve-lammah] have-I-not [lo] found [matsati] favor [khen] in-your-eyes [be-einekha], to-put [lasum] [et] the-burden of [massa] all [kol] this [ha-zeh] people [ha-am] upon-me [alai]?

> **Key terms:** Moses' complaint to YHWH is remarkably bold. הֲרֵעֹתָ (hare'ota) = "have you done evil to" — the hiphil of רעע, addressed directly to YHWH. מַשָּׂא (massa) = "burden/load" — from נשׂא (nasa', "to carry/bear"). Moses frames leadership as an unbearable weight placed upon him. The questions are not rhetorical but genuine expressions of pastoral exhaustion. **LXX:** ἵνα τί ἐκάκωσας τὸν θεράποντά σου ... ἐπιθεῖναι τὴν ὁρμὴν παντὸς τοῦ λαοῦ τούτου ἐπ᾽ ἐμέ ("why have you afflicted your servant ... to place the impulse/burden of all this people upon me") — rendering מַשָּׂא as ὁρμή (hormē, "impulse/rush/burden").

**12** Did-I [he-anokhi] conceive [hariti] [et] all [kol] this [ha-zeh] people [ha-am]? Or-did-I [im-anokhi] give-birth-to-him [yelidtihu], that [ki] you-say [tomar] unto-me [elai]: 'Carry-him [sa'ehu] in-your-bosom [be-kheiqekha], as [ka-asher] carries [yissa] the-nursing-one [ha-omen] [et] the-suckling [ha-yoneq], unto [al] the-ground [ha-adamah] that [asher] you-swore [nishba'ta] to-his-fathers [la-avotav]'?

> **Key terms:** Moses employs startling maternal imagery: הָרִיתִי (hariti) = "did I conceive" — from הרה (harah, "to conceive/be pregnant"); יְלִדְתִּיהוּ (yelidtihu) = "did I give birth to him" — from ילד (yalad, "to bear/give birth"). The אֹמֵן (omen) = "nursing-father/foster-parent" who carries the יוֹנֵק (yoneq) = "suckling/nursing infant" in his חֵיק (kheiq) = "bosom/lap." Moses protests that the parental role belongs to YHWH, not to him. The destination — הָאֲדָמָה אֲשֶׁר נִשְׁבַּעְתָּ לַאֲבֹתָיו (ha-adamah asher nishba'ta la-avotav) = "the ground that you swore to his fathers" — refers to the promised land. **LXX:** μὴ ἐγὼ ἐν γαστρὶ ἔλαβον πάντα τὸν λαὸν τοῦτον, ἢ ἐγὼ ἔτεκον αὐτούς ("did I conceive in [the] womb all this people, or did I give birth to them?").

**13** From-where [me-ayin] to-me [li] flesh [basar] to-give [latet] to-all [le-khol] this [ha-zeh] people [ha-am]? For [ki] they-weep [yivku] upon-me [alai], saying [le'mor]: 'Give [tenah] to-us [lanu] flesh [basar], and-we-will-eat [ve-nokhelah].'

> **Key terms:** מֵאַיִן (me-ayin) = "from where?" — Moses' question expresses impossibility, not ignorance. The demand for flesh/meat (בָּשָׂר, basar) echoes v. 4. The people's weeping upon Moses (עָלַי, alai) = "upon me" conveys the weight of their demand pressing down on him. **LXX:** πόθεν μοι κρέα δοῦναι παντὶ τῷ λαῷ τούτῳ ("from where [shall I get] meat to give to all this people?").

**14** Not [lo] I-am-able [ukhal] I [anokhi] alone [levaddi] to-carry [laset] [et] all [kol] this [ha-zeh] people [ha-am], for [ki] heavy [kaved] [it is] from-me [mimmeni]."

> **Key terms:** כָּבֵד מִמֶּנִּי (kaved mimmeni) = "heavy/too heavy from me" — i.e., too heavy for me. The adjective כָּבֵד (kaved) = "heavy/weighty" — the same root as כָּבוֹד (kavod, "glory/weight"). The weight of the people is too great for one man to bear. This complaint parallels Jethro's earlier advice in Exodus 18:18: "the thing is too heavy for you; you are not able to do it alone." **LXX:** οὐ δυνήσομαι ἐγὼ μόνος φέρειν τὸν λαὸν τοῦτον, ὅτι βαρύτερόν μοί ἐστιν τὸ ῥῆμα τοῦτο ("I will not be able alone to carry this people, for this thing is too heavy for me").

**15** And-if [ve-im] thus [kakhah] you [at] are-doing [oseh] to-me [li], kill-me [horgeni] now [na] — killing [harog] — if [im] I-have-found [matsati] favor [khen] in-your-eyes [be-einekha], and-let-me-not [ve-al] see [er'eh] in-my-evil [be-ra'ati]."

> **Key terms:** הָרְגֵנִי נָא הָרֹג (horgeni na harog) = "kill me, please — killing" — the infinitive absolute הָרֹג (harog) intensifies the imperative: "utterly kill me." Moses' death-wish is the extremity of his despair. The phrase אִם־מָצָאתִי חֵן בְּעֵינֶיךָ (im matsati khen be-einekha) = "if I have found favor in your eyes" — paradoxically, Moses appeals to YHWH's favor as grounds for being put to death. בְּרָעָתִי (be-ra'ati) = "in my evil/misery" — ra'ah here means "misery, wretchedness," not moral evil. Moses asks not to witness his own continued suffering. **LXX:** εἰ οὕτως σὺ ποιεῖς μοι, ἀπόκτεινόν με ἀναιρέσει ... ἵνα μὴ ἴδω μου τὴν κάκωσιν ("if thus you do to me, kill me by slaughter ... that I may not see my affliction").

---

## YHWH's Response: The Seventy Elders and the Promise of Meat (vv. 16–23)

**16** And-said [va-yomer] YHWH unto [el] Moses [Mosheh]: "Gather [esfah] to-me [li] seventy [shiv'im] men [ish] from-the-elders of [mi-ziqnei] Israel [Yisra'el], whom [asher] you-know [yadata] that [ki] they [hem] [are] elders of [ziqnei] the-people [ha-am] and-its-officers [ve-shoteriv]; and-you-shall-take [ve-laqakhta] [et]-them [otam] unto [el] the-Tent of [Ohel] Meeting [Mo'ed], and-they-shall-station-themselves [ve-hityatsvu] there [sham] with-you [immakh].

> **Key terms:** שִׁבְעִים אִישׁ (shiv'im ish) = "seventy men" — the number seventy recurs as a number of fullness in Israel's governance: seventy elders ascended Sinai (Exod 24:1, 9), Jacob's household numbered seventy (Gen 46:27), and seventy nations populate the earth (Gen 10). מִזִּקְנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל (mi-ziqnei Yisra'el) = "from the elders of Israel" — these are not newly appointed but selected from the existing leadership. שֹׁטְרָיו (shoteriv) = "its officers" — שׁוֹטֵר (shoter) denotes a subordinate official, possibly a scribe or foreman (cf. Exod 5:6, 14–15, where the same word describes Israelite foremen under Egyptian taskmasters). **LXX:** συνάγαγέ μοι ἑβδομήκοντα ἄνδρας ἀπὸ τῶν πρεσβυτέρων Ισραηλ ("gather to me seventy men from the elders of Israel").

**17** And-I-will-come-down [ve-yaradti] and-I-will-speak [ve-dibbarti] with-you [immekha] there [sham], and-I-will-take [ve-atsalti] from [min] the-spirit [ha-ruakh] that [asher] [is] upon-you [alekha], and-I-will-put [ve-samti] upon-them [aleihem]; and-they-shall-carry [ve-nas'u] with-you [ittekha] in-the-burden of [be-massa] the-people [ha-am], and-not [ve-lo] you-shall-carry [tissa] you [attah] alone [levaddekha].

> **Key terms:** וְאָצַלְתִּי (ve-atsalti) = "and I will take aside/set apart" — from אצל (atsal), which in the hiphil means "to reserve, set aside, take from." YHWH will take מִן־הָרוּחַ (min ha-ruakh) = "from the spirit" — the partitive מִן ("from/some of") is significant: not all of the spirit is transferred, but a portion is distributed. This does not diminish Moses' portion but extends prophetic capacity to others. The answer to Moses' complaint in v. 14 ("I am not able alone to carry") is here resolved: וְנָשְׂאוּ אִתְּךָ (ve-nas'u ittekha) = "and they shall carry with you." **LXX:** καὶ ἀφελῶ ἀπὸ τοῦ πνεύματος τοῦ ἐπὶ σοί, καὶ ἐπιθήσω ἐπ᾽ αὐτούς ("and I will take from the spirit that [is] upon you, and I will put [it] upon them").

**18** And-unto [ve-el] the-people [ha-am] you-shall-say [tomar]: 'Sanctify-yourselves [hitqaddeshu] for-tomorrow [le-makhar], and-you-shall-eat [va-akhaltem] flesh [basar]; for [ki] you-wept [bekhitem] in-the-ears of [be-oznei] YHWH, saying [le'mor]: "Who [mi] will-feed-us [ya'akhilenu] flesh [basar]? For [ki] [it was] good [tov] for-us [lanu] in-Egypt [be-Mitsrayim]" — and-will-give [ve-natan] YHWH to-you [lakhem] flesh [basar], and-you-shall-eat [va-akhaltem].

> **Key terms:** הִתְקַדְּשׁוּ (hitqaddeshu) = "sanctify yourselves" — the hitpael imperative of קדשׁ (qadash). The command to sanctify before a theophany or divine act is standard (cf. Josh 3:5; 1 Sam 16:5). The irony is sharp: they must prepare themselves in holiness for what will prove to be an act of judgment. כִּי טוֹב לָנוּ בְּמִצְרָיִם (ki tov lanu be-Mitsrayim) = "for [it was] good for us in Egypt" — YHWH quotes back their words, exposing the ingratitude. **LXX:** ἁγιάσθητε εἰς αὔριον, καὶ φάγεσθε κρέα ("sanctify yourselves for tomorrow, and you shall eat meat").

**19** Not [lo] a-day [yom] one [ekhad] you-shall-eat [tokhelun], and-not [ve-lo] two [yomayim] days, and-not [ve-lo] five [khamishah] days [yamim], and-not [ve-lo] ten [asarah] days [yamim], and-not [ve-lo] twenty [esrim] days [yom];

> **Key terms:** The escalating numerical sequence — one, two, five, ten, twenty — builds rhetorical intensity. Each number is negated: the eating of meat will exceed all expectation. The sequence is not strictly mathematical but rhetorically cumulative, driving toward the climax in the next verse. **LXX:** οὐχ ἡμέραν μίαν φάγεσθε οὐδὲ δύο οὐδὲ πέντε ἡμέρας οὐδὲ δέκα ἡμέρας οὐδὲ εἴκοσι ἡμέρας ("not one day shall you eat, nor two, nor five days, nor ten days, nor twenty days").

**20** Until [ad] a-month of [khodesh] days [yamim], until [ad] it-comes-out [yetse] from-your-nostrils [me-appekhem], and-it-becomes [ve-hayah] to-you [lakhem] as-a-loathsome-thing [le-zara]; because [ya'an ki] you-have-rejected [me'astem] [et] YHWH who [asher] [is] in-your-midst [be-qirbekhem], and-you-wept [va-tivku] before-him [lefanav], saying [le'mor]: 'Why [lammah] this [zeh] did-we-come-out [yatsanu] from-Egypt [mi-Mitsrayim]?'"

> **Key terms:** חֹדֶשׁ יָמִים (khodesh yamim) = "a month of days" — i.e., a full month. עַד אֲשֶׁר־יֵצֵא מֵאַפְּכֶם (ad asher yetse me-appekhem) = "until it comes out from your nostrils" — a visceral image of saturation and revulsion. לְזָרָא (le-zara) = "as a loathsome/repulsive thing" — from זור (zur, "to be strange/loathsome"); the noun indicates something that causes physical revulsion. מְאַסְתֶּם (me'astem) = "you have rejected" — from מאס (ma'as, "to reject/despise"). YHWH identifies the complaint about food as a rejection of himself. **LXX:** ἕως μηνὸς ἡμερῶν, ἕως ἂν ἐξέλθῃ ἐκ τῶν μυκτήρων ὑμῶν ("until a month of days, until it comes out from your nostrils") — μυκτήρ (muktēr, "nostril") for אַף.

**21** And-said [va-yomer] Moses [Mosheh]: "Six [shesh] hundred [me'ot] thousand [elef] on-foot [ragli] the-people [ha-am] that [asher] I [anokhi] [am] in-their-midst [be-qirbo]; and-you [ve-attah] have-said [amarta]: 'Flesh [basar] I-will-give [etten] to-them [lahem], and-they-shall-eat [ve-akhelu] a-month of [khodesh] days [yamim].'

> **Key terms:** שֵׁשׁ־מֵאוֹת אֶלֶף רַגְלִי (shesh me'ot elef ragli) = "six hundred thousand on foot" — the census figure from Numbers 1:46 (603,550 fighting men). רַגְלִי (ragli) = "on foot/foot-soldier" — denoting the adult male population. Moses' skepticism focuses on logistics: how can meat feed over six hundred thousand men for a full month? This is not disbelief in YHWH's power but amazement at the scale. **LXX:** ἑξακόσιαι χιλιάδες πεζῶν ὁ λαός ("six hundred thousands of foot-soldiers [are] the people").

**22** Will-flocks [ha-tson] and-herds [u-vaqar] be-slaughtered [yishshakhet] for-them [lahem], and-will-it-suffice [u-matsa] for-them [lahem]? Or [im] [et] all [kol] the-fish of [degei] the-sea [ha-yam] will-be-gathered [ye'asef] for-them [lahem], and-will-it-suffice [u-matsa] for-them [lahem]?"

> **Key terms:** Moses poses two rhetorical questions: (1) If all the flocks (צֹאן, tson = "sheep/goats") and herds (בָּקָר, baqar = "cattle") were slaughtered, would it suffice? (2) If all the fish of the sea were gathered, would it suffice? וּמָצָא (u-matsa) = "and will it find/suffice" — from מצא (matsa') in the sense of "to be sufficient, reach" (cf. Judg 21:14). Moses cannot conceive of a natural source sufficient for such a multitude. **LXX:** μὴ πρόβατα καὶ βόες σφαγήσονται αὐτοῖς ... ἢ πᾶν τὸ ὄψος τῆς θαλάσσης συναχθήσεται αὐτοῖς ("shall sheep and oxen be slaughtered for them ... or shall all the fish of the sea be gathered for them?").

**23** And-said [va-yomer] YHWH unto [el] Moses [Mosheh]: "Is-the-hand of [ha-yad] YHWH too-short [tiqtsar]? Now [attah] you-shall-see [tir'eh] whether-will-happen [ha-yiqrekha] my-word [devari] to-you [a] or [im] not [lo]."

> **Key terms:** הֲיַד יְהוָה תִּקְצָר (ha-yad YHWH tiqtsar) = "is the hand of YHWH too short?" — from קצר (qatsar, "to be short/insufficient"). The "hand" (יָד, yad) is a metonym for power/ability. A "short hand" means insufficient power — an impossibility for YHWH (cf. Isa 50:2; 59:1). הֲיִקְרְךָ דְבָרִי (ha-yiqrekha devari) = "will my word happen to you" — from קרה (qarah, "to encounter/happen/befall"). YHWH's word is not mere speech but an event that will overtake them. **LXX:** μὴ χεὶρ κυρίου οὐκ ἐξαρκέσει; ἤδη γνώσῃ εἰ ἐπικαταλήμψεταί σε ὁ λόγος μου ἢ οὔ ("is the hand of [the] Lord not sufficient? Now you shall know whether my word will overtake you or not").

---

## The Spirit upon the Seventy Elders (vv. 24–30)

**24** And-went-out [va-yetse] Moses [Mosheh] and-spoke [va-yedabber] unto [el] the-people [ha-am] [et] the-words of [divrei] YHWH; and-gathered [va-ye'esof] seventy [shiv'im] men [ish] from-the-elders of [mi-ziqnei] the-people [ha-am], and-stationed [va-ya'amed] [et]-them [otam] around [sevivot] the-Tent [ha-Ohel].

> **Key terms:** סְבִיבוֹת הָאֹהֶל (sevivot ha-Ohel) = "around the Tent" — the seventy elders are positioned in a circle around the Tent of Meeting, receiving their investiture at the sacred center of the camp. The verb וַיַּאֲסֹף (va-ye'esof) = "and he gathered" — from אסף, the same root underlying אֲסַפְסֻף (asafsuf, "rabble") in v. 4, creating an ironic verbal echo: the rabble's chaotic "gathering" is answered by Moses' ordered gathering of the elders. **LXX:** καὶ συνήγαγεν ἑβδομήκοντα ἄνδρας ἀπὸ τῶν πρεσβυτέρων τοῦ λαοῦ, καὶ ἔστησεν αὐτοὺς κύκλῳ τῆς σκηνῆς ("and he gathered seventy men from the elders of the people, and stationed them around the tent").

**25** And-came-down [va-yered] YHWH in-the-cloud [ba-anan], and-spoke [va-yedabber] unto-him [elav]; and-he-took [va-yatsel] from [min] the-spirit [ha-ruakh] that [asher] [was] upon-him [alav], and-gave [va-yitten] upon [al] the-seventy [shiv'im] men [ish] the-elders [ha-zeqenim]; and-it-was [va-yehi] when-rested [ke-no'akh] upon-them [aleihem] the-spirit [ha-ruakh], and-they-prophesied [va-yitnabe'u], and-not [ve-lo] they-continued [yasafu].

> **Key terms:** וַיָּאצֶל (va-yatsel) = "and he took aside/set apart" — fulfilling the promise of v. 17. The spirit is not created anew but distributed from Moses' portion — yet without diminishing Moses. The classical midrash compares this to a candle from which many candles are lit without diminishing the original flame (Sifrei Numbers 93). וַיִּתְנַבְּאוּ (va-yitnabe'u) = "and they prophesied" — the hitpael of נבא, denoting ecstatic prophetic speech. וְלֹא יָסָפוּ (ve-lo yasafu) = "and they did not continue [to do so]" — from יסף (yasaf, "to add/continue"). The prophesying was a one-time confirmatory sign, not an ongoing gift. Alternatively, some read this as "they did not cease" (from סוף, suf, "to cease"), meaning the prophesying continued — but the MT vocalization supports "they did not continue/add." **LXX:** καὶ ἐπροφήτευσαν ἐν τῇ παρεμβολῇ καὶ οὐκέτι προσέθεντο ("and they prophesied in the camp and no longer continued") — supporting the reading "they did not continue."

**26** And-remained [va-yishsha'aru] two [shnei] men [anashim] in-the-camp [ba-makhaneh]; the-name of [shem] the-one [ha-ekhad] Eldad [Eldad], and-the-name of [ve-shem] the-second [ha-sheni] Medad [Medad]; and-rested [va-tanakh] upon-them [aleihem] the-spirit [ha-ruakh] — and-they [ve-hemmah] [were] among-the-ones-written [ba-ketuvim], and-not [ve-lo] they-went-out [yats'u] unto [el] the-Tent [ha-Ohelah]; and-they-prophesied [va-yitnabe'u] in-the-camp [ba-makhaneh].

> **Key terms:** וַיִּשָּׁאֲרוּ (va-yishsha'aru) = "and they remained" — from שׁאר (sha'ar, "to remain/be left over"). Eldad and Medad stayed in the camp rather than going to the Tent. בַּכְּתֻבִים (ba-ketuvim) = "among the ones written" — they were on the official list of the seventy but did not go out to the Tent. The reason is not stated; the text simply notes their absence from the gathering and the remarkable fact that the spirit rested on them regardless. Their names — אֶלְדָּד (Eldad, "God has loved") and מֵידָד (Medad, "Beloved" or "Love") — both derive from roots related to love/affection. **LXX:** καὶ κατελείφθησαν δύο ἄνδρες ἐν τῇ παρεμβολῇ ... καὶ ἐπροφήτευσαν ἐν τῇ παρεμβολῇ ("and two men were left behind in the camp ... and they prophesied in the camp").

**27** And-ran [va-yarats] the-young-man [ha-na'ar] and-told [va-yagged] to-Moses [le-Mosheh], and-said [va-yomer]: "Eldad [Eldad] and-Medad [u-Medad] are-prophesying [mitnabe'im] in-the-camp [ba-makhaneh]."

> **Key terms:** הַנַּעַר (ha-na'ar) = "the young man" — an unnamed youth who serves as a messenger. The verb וַיָּרָץ (va-yarats) = "and he ran" suggests urgency, as if unsanctioned prophecy in the camp were an emergency. **LXX:** καὶ προσδραμὼν ὁ νεανίσκος ἀπήγγειλεν Μωυσῇ ("and the young man, having run up, reported to Moses").

**28** And-answered [va-ya'an] Joshua [Yehoshua] son of [bin] Nun [Nun], the-attendant of [mesharet] Moses [Mosheh], from-his-chosen-ones [mi-bekhurav], and-said [va-yomer]: "My-lord [adoni] Moses [Mosheh], restrain-them [kela'em]!"

> **Key terms:** יְהוֹשֻׁעַ בִּן־נוּן (Yehoshua bin Nun) = "Joshua son of Nun" — identified as מְשָׁרֵת מֹשֶׁה (mesharet Mosheh) = "the attendant/minister of Moses." The phrase מִבְּחֻרָיו (mi-bekhurav) = "from his chosen ones" — i.e., one of those chosen from his youth, or one of Moses' select attendants. The Masoretic vocalization points to בָּחוּר (bakhur, "chosen/select young man") rather than בְּחִירָה (bekhirah, "choosing"). כְּלָאֵם (kela'em) = "restrain them/shut them up" — from כלא (kala', "to restrain/imprison/withhold"). Joshua perceives unauthorized prophecy as a threat to Moses' unique authority. **LXX:** κύριε Μωυσῆ, κώλυσον αὐτούς ("my lord Moses, hinder/prevent them").

**29** And-said [va-yomer] to-him [lo] Moses [Mosheh]: "Are-you-jealous [ha-meqanne] you [attah] for-me [li]? And-who-would-give [u-mi yitten] [that] all [kol] the-people of [am] YHWH [were] prophets [nevi'im], that [ki] would-give [yitten] YHWH [et] his-spirit [rukho] upon-them [aleihem]!"

> **Key terms:** הַמְקַנֵּא אַתָּה לִי (ha-meqanne attah li) = "are you jealous for me?" — the piel participle of קנא (qana', "to be jealous/zealous"). Moses rebukes Joshua's protectiveness. The wish-formula וּמִי יִתֵּן (u-mi yitten) = "and who would give" (= "would that!") expresses Moses' desire that all of YHWH's people could be prophets. This is one of the most expansive statements about the spirit in the Torah — Moses envisions universal prophetic endowment, a vision echoed by Joel 3:1 [Eng. 2:28]: "I will pour out my spirit on all flesh." **LXX:** μὴ ζηλοῖς σύ μοι; καὶ τίς δῴη πάντα τὸν λαὸν κυρίου προφήτας ("are you jealous for me? And who would grant all the people of [the] Lord [to be] prophets?").

**30** And-gathered [va-ye'asef] Moses [Mosheh] unto [el] the-camp [ha-makhaneh], he [hu] and-the-elders of [ve-ziqnei] Israel [Yisra'el].

> **Key terms:** Moses and the seventy elders return from the Tent of Meeting to the camp. The verb וַיֵּאָסֶף (va-ye'asef) = "and he gathered himself" (niphal of אסף) — i.e., he withdrew back into the camp. This transitional verse closes the elder-investiture scene and prepares for the quail narrative. **LXX:** καὶ ἀπῆλθεν Μωυσῆς εἰς τὴν παρεμβολήν ("and Moses departed into the camp").

---

## The Quail and the Plague (vv. 31–35)

**31** And-a-wind [ve-ruakh] journeyed [nasa] from-with [me-et] YHWH, and-cut-off [va-yagaz] quail [salvav] from [min] the-sea [ha-yam]; and-cast [va-yittosh] upon [al] the-camp [ha-makhaneh] as-a-journey of [ke-derekh] a-day [yom] thus [koh], and-as-a-journey of [u-khe-derekh] a-day [yom] thus [koh], all-around [sevivot] the-camp [ha-makhaneh]; and-about-two-cubits [u-khe-ammatayim] upon [al] the-face of [penei] the-ground [ha-arets].

> **Key terms:** רוּחַ (ruakh) = "wind/spirit" — the same word used for the prophetic spirit in vv. 17–29; here it is a physical wind sent by YHWH. וַיָּגָז (va-yagaz) = "and it cut off/drove" — from גוז (guz, "to cut off, pass over"), a rare verb. שַׂלְוָו (salvav) = "quail" — the שְׂלָו (selav) is a migratory quail (Coturnix coturnix) that crosses the Sinai region in large flocks during spring and autumn migrations. מִן־הַיָּם (min ha-yam) = "from the sea" — either the Mediterranean or the Gulf of Aqaba/Red Sea. כְּדֶרֶךְ יוֹם כֹּה וּכְדֶרֶךְ יוֹם כֹּה (ke-derekh yom koh u-khe-derekh yom koh) = "about a day's journey this way and about a day's journey that way" — the quail covered an enormous area in every direction around the camp. וּכְאַמָּתַיִם (u-khe-ammatayim) = "and about two cubits" (approximately three feet / one meter) — this describes the depth of quail on the ground, or their low flight altitude. **LXX:** καὶ πνεῦμα ἐξῆλθεν παρὰ κυρίου καὶ ἐξεπέρασεν ὀρτυγομήτραν ἀπὸ τῆς θαλάσσης ("and a wind went out from [the] Lord and drove quail from the sea") — ὀρτυγομήτρα (ortugomētra) is the specific Greek term for migratory quail.

**32** And-rose [va-yaqam] the-people [ha-am] all [kol] that [ha-hu] day [ha-yom] and-all [ve-khol] the-night [ha-lailah] and-all [ve-khol] the-day of [yom] the-morrow [ha-makhorat], and-gathered [va-ya'asfu] [et] the-quail [ha-selav]; the-one-who-did-least [ha-mam'it] gathered [asaf] ten [asarah] homers [khomarim]; and-they-spread [va-yishtetkhu] for-themselves [lahem] spreading [shatoakh] around [sevivot] the-camp [ha-makhaneh].

> **Key terms:** The people gathered for thirty-six consecutive hours — all day, all night, and all the next day. הַמַּמְעִיט (ha-mam'it) = "the one who did least" — the hiphil participle of מעט (ma'at, "to be little/few"). Even the minimum gatherer collected ten חֳמָרִים (khomarim) = "homers" — a homer is approximately 220 liters / 6.3 bushels, so ten homers equals roughly 2,200 liters, an enormous quantity. וַיִּשְׁטְחוּ לָהֶם שָׁטוֹחַ (va-yishtetkhu lahem shatoakh) = "and they spread [them] out for themselves spreading" — a cognate infinitive construction intensifying the action. The quail were spread out to dry in the sun for preservation. **LXX:** ὁ τὸ ὀλίγον συνήγαγεν δέκα κόρους ("the one [who gathered] little gathered ten cors") — κόρος (koros) = Hebrew חֹמֶר (khomer).

**33** The-flesh [ha-basar] was-still [odenu] between [bein] their-teeth [shinneihem], before [terem] it-was-cut-off [yikkaret]; and-the-anger of [ve-af] YHWH burned [kharah] against-the-people [ba-am], and-struck [va-yakh] YHWH among-the-people [ba-am] a-plague [makkah] exceedingly [me'od] great [rabbah].

> **Key terms:** עוֹדֶנּוּ בֵּין שִׁנֵּיהֶם (odenu bein shinneihem) = "still between their teeth" — the meat had not yet been chewed and swallowed, let alone digested. טֶרֶם יִכָּרֵת (terem yikkaret) = "before it was cut off" — i.e., before the supply was exhausted, or before they had finished eating. The phrase may also mean "before it could be chewed off." מַכָּה רַבָּה מְאֹד (makkah rabbah me'od) = "an exceedingly great plague/striking" — from נכה (nakhah, "to strike") and מַכָּה (makkah, "blow/plague/striking"). The plague struck while the meat was still in their mouths — divine judgment was immediate. **LXX:** τὰ κρέα ἔτι ἦν ἐν τοῖς ὀδοῦσιν αὐτῶν, πρὶν ἢ ἐκλεῖψαι, καὶ κύριος ἐθυμώθη εἰς τὸν λαόν, καὶ ἐπάταξεν κύριος τὸν λαὸν πληγὴν μεγάλην σφόδρα ("the meat was still in their teeth, before [it] failed, and [the] Lord was angry with the people, and [the] Lord struck the people [with] an exceedingly great plague").

**34** And-he-called [va-yiqra] the-name of [shem] that [ha-hu] place [ha-maqom] Qivrot-ha-Ta'avah [Qivrot ha-Ta'avah], for [ki] there [sham] they-buried [qaveru] [et] the-people [ha-am] the-ones-craving [ha-mit'avvim].

> **Key terms:** קִבְרוֹת הַתַּאֲוָה (Qivrot ha-Ta'avah) = "Graves of the Craving" — from קֶבֶר (qever, "grave") and תַּאֲוָה (ta'avah, "craving/desire"). The name memorializes the greed that brought death. הַמִּתְאַוִּים (ha-mit'avvim) = "the ones craving" — the hitpael participle of אוה (avah, "to desire/crave"), the same root as the cognate accusative in v. 4 (הִתְאַוּוּ תַּאֲוָה). The cycle is complete: craving (v. 4) → graves (v. 34). **LXX:** καὶ ἐκλήθη τὸ ὄνομα τοῦ τόπου ἐκείνου Μνήματα τῆς Ἐπιθυμίας ("and the name of that place was called 'Graves of the Desire'") — again translating rather than transliterating the Hebrew place-name.

**35** From-Qivrot-ha-Ta'avah [mi-Qivrot ha-Ta'avah] the-people [ha-am] journeyed [nas'u] to-Khatserot [Khatserot]; and-they-were [va-yihyu] in-Khatserot [ba-Khatserot].

> **Key terms:** חֲצֵרוֹת (Khatserot) = "Enclosures/Courtyards" — the next campsite, from חָצֵר (khatser, "court/enclosure"). This place becomes the setting for the Miriam narrative in chapter 12. The itinerary note is sparse: departure, arrival, and settlement — the standard formula of the wilderness travel notices (cf. Num 33:16–17). **LXX:** ἀπὸ Μνημάτων τῆς Ἐπιθυμίας ἐξῆρεν ὁ λαὸς εἰς Ασηρωθ, καὶ ἐγένετο ὁ λαὸς ἐν Ασηρωθ ("from Graves of the Desire the people set out to Haseroth, and the people were in Haseroth") — the LXX transliterates חֲצֵרוֹת as Ασηρωθ (Asērōth).

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

### Manuscript Observations from the Leningrad Codex (pp. 160–162)

1. **Chapter boundary and parashah structure:** Numbers 11 begins on page 160 of the Leningrad Codex, immediately following the inverted-nun passage of 10:35–36. An open parashah marker (פ) appears before v. 4, creating a clear division between the brief Taberah incident (vv. 1–3) and the extended quail-and-elders narrative (vv. 4–35). The chapter extends through page 161 and onto page 162, where it concludes with the itinerary notice of v. 35. A setumah (ס) appears at the chapter's end before 12:1.

2. **The hapax legomenon מִתְאֹנְנִים (v. 1):** The word מִתְאֹנְנִים (mit'onenim) occurs only here in the Hebrew Bible. The Leningrad Codex writes it clearly with the hitpoel pattern. The root אנן is uncertain — it may relate to אוֹן (on, "sorrow/trouble") or to אָנָה (anah, "to mourn/groan"). The LXX's γογγύζων (gonguzōn, "murmuring") represents an interpretive rendering, connecting this rare word to the broader Exodus complaint tradition. Targum Onqelos renders it as מִתְרַעֲמִין (mitraameen, "ones grumbling"), similarly interpretive.

3. **The אֲסַפְסֻף (asafsuf) in v. 4:** This reduplicated noun is another hapax legomenon, clearly legible in the Leningrad Codex. The scribal hand shows no hesitation or correction, confirming the received form. The doubled consonant pattern (אסף + סף) is an intensive/pejorative formation, paralleling other Hebrew reduplicated forms that denote contempt or excess.

4. **Moses' maternal language (v. 12):** The verbs הָרִיתִי ("did I conceive") and יְלִדְתִּיהוּ ("did I give birth to him") are vocalized without ambiguity in the Leningrad Codex. The first-person forms applying birthing language to a male speaker (Moses) are striking and deliberately subversive — the text makes Moses speak in the voice of a mother to emphasize that parental responsibility for Israel belongs to YHWH, not to Moses.

5. **The numeral sequence in v. 19:** The escalating numbers (one, two, five, ten, twenty) are written in full lexical form, not abbreviated. The Leningrad Codex spacing shows each clause as a distinct rhythmic unit, creating the effect of accumulating emphasis before the climax in v. 20 (a full month).

### Observations from Codex Vaticanus (pp. 120–121)

6. **LXX translation of place-names:** The Vaticanus consistently translates rather than transliterates the etiological place-names: תַּבְעֵרָה becomes Ἐμπυρισμός ("Burning") in v. 3, and קִבְרוֹת הַתַּאֲוָה becomes Μνήματα τῆς Ἐπιθυμίας ("Graves of the Desire") in v. 34. This contrasts with חֲצֵרוֹת in v. 35, which is transliterated as Ασηρωθ. The pattern suggests the LXX translator rendered names whose etymology was narratively significant (fire → "Burning"; craving → "Graves of Desire") but transliterated names whose etymology was less immediately relevant to the story.

7. **LXX rendering of בְּדֹלַח as κρύσταλλος (v. 7):** The Vaticanus reads κρύσταλλος ("crystal") where the MT has בְּדֹלַח (bedolakh, "bdellium"). This is a significant interpretive divergence. The MT compares the manna's appearance to a translucent resin; the LXX compares it to crystal or ice. Both convey translucency and pale coloring, but the LXX version suggests greater hardness and brightness. This may reflect the LXX translator's unfamiliarity with bdellium or a deliberate exegetical choice to emphasize the manna's otherworldly brilliance.

8. **LXX ὁρμή for מַשָּׂא in v. 11:** The Vaticanus renders Moses' complaint about the "burden" (מַשָּׂא, massa) of the people as ὁρμή (hormē, "impulse/rush/onset"). This is an unusual equivalence. The standard LXX rendering of מַשָּׂא is βάσταγμα or φορτίον ("burden/load"). The choice of ὁρμή suggests the translator understood Moses' burden not as dead weight but as the relentless pressure/onslaught of the people's demands — a more dynamic image than simple heaviness.

9. **Vaticanus paragraph division at the elder-investiture:** The Vaticanus shows a slight spacing before the narrative of the seventy elders (v. 16), corresponding to the shift from the people's complaint to YHWH's response. This structural division aligns with the MT's parashah structure but is expressed through spatial arrangement rather than explicit markers.

### Theological and Structural Notes

10. **The three complaints and three responses:** Numbers 11 contains a carefully structured pattern of complaint and response: (a) The people complain about hardship → YHWH sends fire → Moses intercedes → fire subsides (vv. 1–3). (b) The people crave meat → YHWH promises meat that will become loathsome → quail come → plague strikes (vv. 4–6, 18–20, 31–34). (c) Moses complains about the burden of leadership → YHWH distributes the spirit upon seventy elders → shared governance is established (vv. 10–15, 16–17, 24–30). The three episodes are not sequential but interlocking: YHWH addresses both the people's complaint (meat) and Moses' complaint (burden) simultaneously, but with radically different outcomes — judgment for the people's ingratitude, relief for Moses' honest desperation.

11. **רוּחַ (ruakh) as wind and spirit:** The word רוּחַ appears in two distinct senses within this chapter: as the divine spirit distributed to the elders (vv. 17, 25–26, 29) and as the physical wind that drives the quail from the sea (v. 31). The dual usage creates a theological wordplay: the same divine רוּחַ that empowers prophetic leadership also controls the natural world. YHWH's ruakh gives wisdom (to the elders) and gives food (to the people), but the reception of each gift produces opposite results — the elders receive the spirit with humility, while the people receive the quail with greed.

12. **Moses' wish and Joel's prophecy (v. 29):** Moses' exclamation — "Would that all the people of YHWH were prophets, that YHWH would give his spirit upon them!" — stands as one of the Torah's most forward-looking theological statements. It envisions a democratization of prophetic experience that transcends the hierarchical mediation of Moses' own role. Joel 3:1 [Eng. 2:28] explicitly takes up this vision: "I will pour out my spirit on all flesh; your sons and your daughters shall prophesy." The author of Acts 2:17–21 cites Joel's text as fulfilled at Pentecost, creating a line from Numbers 11:29 through Joel to the early church's experience of universal spiritual endowment. Moses himself, burdened to the point of requesting death, is the one who voices the hope of universal prophecy — his lowest moment produces his most expansive vision.

13. **The etiology of Qivrot ha-Ta'avah and the anatomy of desire:** The name "Graves of the Craving" does not say "Graves of the Ones Who Craved" but "Graves of the Craving [itself]" — it is the desire that is buried, not merely the desirers. The Ta'avah (craving) is treated as an entity that consumed its hosts and was then interred with them. The cognate accusative of v. 4 (הִתְאַוּוּ תַּאֲוָה, "they craved a craving") presents desire as an object external to the self that overtakes and devours — a personification that the place-name memorializes permanently. The wilderness geography becomes a moral map: Taberah ("Burning") marks the location of divine anger; Qivrot ha-Ta'avah ("Graves of Craving") marks the location of human appetite; and Khatserot ("Enclosures") — the next stop — will become the place of Miriam's challenge to Moses' authority (chapter 12). Each station names a crisis.
